agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Integration of two previously independent bounded contexts — Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management — to enforce stock availability at order placement time. This is a fundamental shift from the current design where orders are placed without regard to stock, implying the business previously handled stock shortages downstream (e.g., backorders, manual review, supplier reorders). The feature request introduces synchronous coupling between contexts that were intentionally decoupled.",
    "bounded_context": "Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management (Context A) and Sales/Order Management (Context B). Neither context currently owns the concept of 'stock sufficiency at order time.' This is an emerging integration concern that must be carefully placed.",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "StockLevel", "definition": "Context A concept: the current physical quantity of a Product in the warehouse. Integer >= 0. Authoritative source of inventory truth." },
      { "term": "Order", "definition": "Context B concept: a customer's intent to purchase one or more products, composed of OrderLines." },
      { "term": "OrderLine", "definition": "Context B concept: a line item referencing a Product by ID and a desired Quantity." },
      { "term": "Product", "definition": "Exists in both contexts but may carry different meaning. Context A: a stockable item with physical inventory. Context B: a sellable item with pricing. These are NOT the same entity — they share an ID." },
      { "term": "Stock Reservation", "definition": "MISSING CONCEPT — the act of temporarily claiming stock for a pending order. Neither context currently owns this. Must be explicitly modeled if blocking is introduced." },
      { "term": "Stock Sufficiency Check", "definition": "PROPOSED CONCEPT — a point-in-time query asking whether Context A can fulfill a requested Quantity. Not currently modeled anywhere." },
      { "term": "Available Stock", "definition": "AMBIGUOUS — does this mean physical StockLevel, or StockLevel minus already-reserved-but-not-yet-picked quantities? The feature request does not distinguish these, and the difference is critical." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "StockLevel for any Product must never go below zero.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Physical constraint — you cannot pick items that do not exist in the warehouse. Violating this corrupts inventory state and cascades into fulfillment failures.",
      "testable_assertion": "For all Products, StockLevel >= 0 at all times, across all concurrent operations."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "An Order may contain multiple OrderLines, each referencing a Product ID and a Quantity > 0.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Structural integrity — an OrderLine with zero or negative quantity is semantically meaningless and would corrupt downstream fulfillment and billing.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every persisted OrderLine has Quantity > 0 and a valid Product ID reference."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "A stock sufficiency check is a point-in-time snapshot and does NOT guarantee stock will be available at pick time.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Temporal consistency constraint. Between order placement and warehouse picking, other orders may consume stock, stock may be damaged, or corrections may occur. Any design that treats the check as a guarantee will produce data corruption under concurrency.",
      "testable_assertion": "The system must not assume that a passed stock sufficiency check eliminates the possibility of insufficient stock at fulfillment time."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "If stock is insufficient for any OrderLine at order placement, the Order must be blocked (rejected or held for review).",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Business policy — the sales team could later decide to allow backorders, partial fulfillment, or priority overrides. A VP could change this threshold or add exceptions.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a Product with StockLevel 5, when an Order is placed with Quantity 10 for that Product, then the Order is blocked and not confirmed."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "The 'stock available' indicator shown to Sales must reflect warehouse state with acceptable staleness, not be treated as a transactional lock.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "UI policy — 'real-time' is a business aspiration, not a physical constraint. The acceptable staleness window (seconds? minutes?) is a tunable business parameter.",
      "testable_assertion": "The stock indicator displayed in the Sales UI is updated within [UNDEFINED — see escalation] seconds of a StockLevel change in Context A."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Context B (Sales) must not directly mutate StockLevel in Context A (Warehouse). Stock changes are owned exclusively by Warehouse Management.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Bounded context sovereignty. If Sales can directly decrement stock, the warehouse loses authoritative control over its own aggregate, breaking inventory auditability and enabling phantom stock states.",
      "testable_assertion": "No write path exists from the Sales/Order context to the StockLevel field in Warehouse Management."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A Product with StockLevel of N in the Warehouse context",
      "when": "A customer places an Order containing an OrderLine for that Product with Quantity <= N",
      "then": "The Order is accepted (not blocked)"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A Product with StockLevel of N in the Warehouse context",
      "when": "A customer places an Order containing an OrderLine for that Product with Quantity > N",
      "then": "The Order is blocked, and the customer and sales team are informed of insufficient stock"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A StockLevel changes in the Warehouse context (via pick or receipt)",
      "when": "The Sales UI is displaying the stock indicator for that Product",
      "then": "The indicator updates within the defined staleness SLA without requiring a page refresh"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "Two customers simultaneously place Orders that together would exceed StockLevel",
      "when": "Both Orders are submitted concurrently",
      "then": "At most one Order is accepted; the other is blocked. StockLevel never goes below zero. No double-selling occurs."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "An Order with multiple OrderLines",
      "when": "Stock is sufficient for some OrderLines but not all",
      "then": "The entire Order is blocked (atomic check) — OR — the system applies a partial fulfillment policy. THIS BEHAVIOR IS UNDEFINED in the spec and must be clarified."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "The Warehouse context is temporarily unavailable",
      "when": "A customer places an Order",
      "then": "The system degrades gracefully — either queuing the order for later validation or informing the customer, but never silently accepting an unvalidated order as confirmed"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Concurrent orders for the same product exhaust stock simultaneously", "expected_behavior": "Only orders that can be fulfilled are accepted. The system must handle race conditions — optimistic locking, reservation pattern, or saga with compensation. StockLevel must never go negative." },
    { "scenario": "Stock indicator shows 'available' but stock is consumed between display and order submission", "expected_behavior": "The blocking check at order time is authoritative, not the UI indicator. Customer sees a clear rejection message, not a silent failure. The indicator was a hint, not a contract." },
    { "scenario": "Multi-line order where one line has sufficient stock and another does not", "expected_behavior": "Business must define: all-or-nothing vs. partial acceptance. This is unspecified and must be escalated." },
    { "scenario": "StockLevel is exactly zero — customer orders Quantity 1", "expected_behavior": "Order is blocked. The indicator should already show 'unavailable.' Edge: does the business want to allow backorders or waitlisting?" },
    { "scenario": "Supplier receipt increases StockLevel while a blocked order exists", "expected_behavior": "Unspecified. Should blocked orders be automatically re-evaluated? Or must the customer re-place? This impacts the meaning of 'blocked.'" },
    { "scenario": "Product exists in Sales context but not yet set up in Warehouse context", "expected_behavior": "The stock check must fail safely. A missing product in Context A is not the same as StockLevel = 0. The system must distinguish 'unknown product' from 'out of stock.'" },
    { "scenario": "Warehouse context is unavailable or network partition occurs during the stock check", "expected_behavior": "The system must choose a failure mode: fail-open (accept order, risk oversell) or fail-closed (block order, risk lost sale). This is a critical business policy decision that is unspecified." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Blocking orders on insufficient stock prevents overselling and the associated costs of cancellations, refunds, expedited shipping, and customer compensation. However, overly aggressive blocking (stale data, no backorder option) may reject valid orders and lose revenue. The concurrency edge case is particularly dangerous — double-selling a scarce item is worse financially than occasionally blocking a valid order.",
    "operational": "This introduces a synchronous dependency between two previously independent contexts. Warehouse system downtime now directly impacts order placement — a failure mode that did not previously exist. Operational teams must define SLAs for the stock query path and plan for degradation. The warehouse team now has an implicit obligation to keep their system highly available for the sales path.",
    "user": "Customers gain transparency (stock indicator) but lose the ability to place orders freely. If the blocking is too aggressive or based on stale data, customer experience degrades. The sales team benefits from real-time visibility but may face increased support inquiries when orders are blocked. Clear messaging on 'why blocked' and 'what next' is essential.",
    "risk": "HIGH. Cross-context coupling is the primary architectural risk. Temporal coupling (synchronous stock check) creates a blast radius where warehouse issues cascade into sales failures. Data consistency risk from eventual consistency — the indicator and the check may disagree. Concurrency risk from race conditions on popular products. Domain model risk from conflating 'physical stock' with 'available-to-promise stock' (reserved vs unreserved).",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Orders may be blocked that previously would have been accepted (even if unfulfillable). Must receive clear, actionable feedback. May lose ability to backorder." },
      { "group": "Sales Team", "impact": "Gains real-time stock visibility. May face customer complaints about blocked orders. Needs training on what 'blocked' means and how to resolve." },
      { "group": "Warehouse Operations", "impact": "Their system now sits on the critical path for order placement. Uptime and performance requirements increase. They must define what 'available stock' means (gross vs. net of reservations)." },
      { "group": "Engineering / Platform", "impact": "Must build a cross-context integration (anti-corruption layer, events, or API). Must handle distributed systems concerns: latency, failure, consistency. Significant new complexity." },
      { "group": "Finance / Accounting", "impact": "Fewer oversell scenarios means fewer refunds and compensation costs. But blocked orders may reduce top-line revenue if not handled with backorder or waitlist options." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 40,
    "reasoning": "The feature request has multiple critical ambiguities: (1) 'Available stock' is undefined — gross StockLevel vs. net-of-reservations, (2) partial fulfillment policy is unspecified, (3) 'real-time' latency SLA is undefined, (4) failure mode during Warehouse unavailability is unspecified, (5) there is no mention of a Reservation concept which is essential to correctness under concurrency, (6) the behavior for re-evaluating blocked orders is undefined. These are not implementation details — they are business policy decisions that fundamentally change the domain model.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "StockLevel >= 0 is an inviolable invariant",
      "Context B must not directly mutate Context A's state",
      "The stock indicator is a hint, not a transactional guarantee",
      "Concurrency control is required to prevent overselling"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Definition of 'available stock' (gross vs. net of reservations)",
      "Partial fulfillment vs. all-or-nothing order blocking policy",
      "Acceptable staleness for the 'real-time' indicator",
      "Failure mode when Warehouse context is unavailable",
      "Whether blocked orders should be automatically re-evaluated when stock is replenished",
      "Whether backorders or waitlisting should be supported"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The current design (orders placed without stock check) was intentional, not an oversight — implying downstream handling of stock shortages exists today",
      "Product IDs are shared between contexts (same identity, different models)",
      "The Warehouse context is the single source of truth for physical inventory",
      "The blocking behavior is for the entire order, not per-line (but this is unconfirmed)"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "'Available stock' is undefined. Does it mean the raw StockLevel (physical count) or StockLevel minus quantities already reserved for other accepted-but-not-yet-picked orders? Without a Reservation concept, two accepted orders can claim the same stock, making the blocking check theater — it prevents nothing under concurrency. This is the single most important design decision in this feature.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Warehouse Operations", "Engineering", "Customers"],
      "options": [
        "A) Available = raw StockLevel (simpler, but allows overselling under concurrency)",
        "B) Available = StockLevel minus reserved quantities (correct, but requires introducing a Reservation aggregate and a new integration pattern)"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B. Without reservations, the blocking check gives false confidence. However, this requires defining reservation lifetime, expiry, and cancellation policies — a non-trivial domain extension."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Multi-line order fulfillment policy is unspecified. When an order has 3 lines and only 2 have sufficient stock, should the entire order be blocked (all-or-nothing) or should partial fulfillment be allowed?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Sales Team", "Warehouse Operations"],
      "options": [
        "A) All-or-nothing: block the entire order if any line is insufficient",
        "B) Partial fulfillment: accept available lines, block or backorder the rest",
        "C) Customer choice: let the customer decide at order time"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A as the safe default (simpler invariant to enforce), with Option C as a future enhancement. But the business must explicitly decide."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "'Real-time' stock indicator has no defined staleness SLA. In a distributed system, true real-time is impossible. Is 1-second staleness acceptable? 30 seconds? 5 minutes? This drives the entire integration architecture (webhooks vs. polling vs. shared read model).",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Sales Team", "Engineering", "Customers"],
      "options": [
        "A) Sub-second (requires event-driven push architecture, significant complexity)",
        "B) 5-30 seconds (event-driven with batching, moderate complexity)",
        "C) 1-5 minutes (polling-based, simple but may mislead sales team)"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B. Sub-second is over-engineering for a human-viewed indicator. Minutes-level staleness defeats the purpose. 5-30 seconds with event-driven updates is the right tradeoff."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Failure mode when the Warehouse context is unavailable during order placement is unspecified. This is a critical business policy decision, not a technical one.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Sales Team", "Engineering"],
      "options": [
        "A) Fail-closed: block all orders when stock cannot be verified (safe, but may lose sales during outages)",
        "B) Fail-open: accept orders without verification, handle oversells downstream (risky, but preserves revenue)",
        "C) Queue-and-validate: accept order tentatively, validate asynchronously, notify customer if blocked after the fact"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C. It preserves the current behavior (orders accepted without stock check) as a graceful degradation path while adding validation when the system is healthy. But the business must accept the risk of delayed blocking."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No existing concept of Stock Reservation exists in either context. The feature request implicitly requires one — without it, accepting an order does not 'claim' stock, meaning a passed check gives no guarantee. Who owns the Reservation? Context A (Warehouse — it's a stock concern) or a new Integration/Fulfillment context? How long does a reservation last? What triggers release?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Warehouse Operations", "Sales Team"],
      "options": [
        "A) Warehouse owns reservations as part of its StockLevel management",
        "B) Introduce a new Fulfillment/Allocation bounded context that mediates between Sales and Warehouse",
        "C) Skip reservations entirely and accept the race condition risk"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A for simplicity if the Warehouse team agrees to own this concept. Option B is more correct for complex organizations but introduces a third context. Option C is unacceptable for any product with meaningful concurrency."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
